name: Herbie Mann
abstract: Herbert Jay Solomon (April 16, 1930 – July 1, 2003), known by his stage name Herbie Mann, was an American jazz flute player and important early practitioner of world music. Early in his career, he also played tenor saxophone and clarinet (including bass clarinet), but Mann was among the first jazz musicians to specialize on the flute.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: From Mingus, to Herbie Mann... I was working with Herbie Mann... So, he called Reggie Workman and Reggie Workman joined the band. So, it was a good band, you know?
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mark Soskin
about_quotes.evidence: I played a lot with Herbie and yeah, at one time I was playing with Herbie... Mainly with Herbie it was playing Brazilian music
about_quotes.doc_title: Mark Soskin interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 17, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: I have to take off for about five weeks because I’m going to Europe, I’m going to Japan and I’m working with Herbie Mann.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Mario Bauza
about_quotes.evidence: So we made an album with Herbie Mann. I made an album with Herbie Mann

about_quotes.speaker: Earl May
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions Herbie Mann in a list of people the interviewee has played with, but the interviewee does not confirm playing with Herbie Mann, instead listing other musicians like Joe Newman, Ted Dunbar, and Frank Foster.
about_quotes.doc_title: Earl May interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 11, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Gary Burton
about_quotes.evidence: Herbie Mann called me, said he’d heard about me, and was thinking about changing vibes players... So he asked me to come by Basin Street East and listen to the band, and then chat.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gary Burton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
